original_abstract: In contemporary society, children's neglect in treating their parents properly is increasingly observed. Filial piety, ranking just below the obligation to worship Allah SWT, is essential and extends universally beyond Muslim parents. This research aims to: 1) Analyze the interpretation of Al-Walid according to Ibn Kathīr and M. Quraish Shihab, and 2) Identify differences and similarities in their exegesis of Surah Al-Isra, verses 23-24. Using qualitative library research, primary sources include the tafsir works of Ibn Kathīr and Al-Mishbah by M. Quraish Shihab, along with related journals, articles, and books. Findings reveal: 1) The commandment to worship Allah SWT exclusively, and 2) The obligation for children to show filial piety by avoiding harsh words, even mild expressions like "ah". Children must speak gently and politely, show reverence, pray for their parents, engage in actions that please them, and avoid actions that displease them. This research highlights the importance of maintaining filial piety under all circumstances and practicing iḥsān, treating others better than they treat us. The study compares interpretations by Ibn Kathīr and M. Quraish Shihab, noting a significant distinction in the term hisanan. Ibn Kathīr sees it as a command to do good to one's parents, while Quraish Shihab attributes two meanings: 1) Bestowing favors upon others, and 2) Performing good deeds. The key principle is treating parents with greater kindness and respect than they extend to us.
humanized_abstract: Neglect in Obeying Commands from Children towards Their Parents Nowadays, there are many children who are lacking in morality, or in other terms, lacking in observing proper manner in dealing with their parents. Something that is meant as a moral foundation for children, is known as “filial piety”, as it is described by Islamic scholars to be one of the important fundamentals of Islam, which is also interpreted as being part of the five basics of Islam that children must obey towards their parents, as it extends not only towards the parents of Muslim children, but also towards the parents of non-Muslim children. Research Objective and Methodology: 1. To describe and explain the concept of Al-Walid that is narrated in the tafseer of Ibn Kathīr and M. Quraish Shihab, and 2. To describe and explain the difference and similarities between the explanation made by Ibn Kathīr and M. Quraish Shihab in Surah Al-Isra verses 23-24 with qualitative library research approach. The literature used in this study were from tafseer written by IBN KATHIR and M QURAISH SHIHAB along with journal, article and book literature. The results obtained from the research questions are: 1. Exclusivity of worship to Allah SWT, 2. Commands on children to observe the rights of their parents which in this case means that children are not allowed to utter harsh words or even what may be deemed mild to the ears of their parents, that is "ah". In short, children should talk with manners and respect to their parents, that they should hold high respect towards their parents and worship them with doing dhikr in the form of supplications, to please their parents by performing any tasks asked for by their parents as well as refraining from doing things that may displease their parents. Thus this research is very meaningful in the sense that children must respect and observe the rights given by Allah to their parents under all situations. It is a must for them to perform iḥsān as an act of boosting their moralities, and as an act of increasing their caringness and treatment to other human beings beyond what other human beings do to them. This study highlights on explanation of term "hisanan" and its different meanings between the explanation made by IBN KATHIR and M. Quraish Shihab. As explained by the former, the term "hisanan" here implies that children have to do good to their parents, while the latter on the other hand explains two of the meanings in the term "hisanan" that are to bestow favours upon other than the parents and performing good deeds. The underlying principle in this context is to command on children to look up to their parents with greater mannerisms and respect that exceeds the mannerisms and respect given by parents towards their children.
